role_summary: First treasurer of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense (Oakland, 1966); the party's first martyr, killed by Oakland police on April 6, 1968, at age 17, two days after Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination.

photo.alt: Bobby Hutton, from a Black Panther Party leaflet circulated after his death
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from an Archive.org collection of 1960s Black Panther Party leaflets)
photo.license: Public domain in the United States (first published in the United States between 1931 and 1977 without a copyright notice)

ai_reading: Hutton's name surfaces in the catalog's Black Panther Party cohort more than any other non-interviewee named figure: his death is part of the biographical material on the pages of Aaron Dixon (entry 1), Elmer Dixon (entry 37), and Elbert "Big Man" Howard (entry 35), and he appears in a gallery caption on the Kathleen Cleaver page (entry 73). Two corpus interviewees give direct verbatim testimony about him: in the transcripts, both Dixon brothers narrate driving across the Bay Bridge to his April 1968 Oakland funeral, and that funeral is the moment each of them dates an organizational turning point to. Hutton himself is not an interviewee in the Civil Rights History Project (his death predated the project by four decades) and does not appear in FamousNames or the influence graph, but his standalone catalog page exists so visitors searching his name find the cluster of corpus voices that mourn and organize around him.
biographical_paragraph: Hutton was born April 21, 1950 in Jefferson County, Arkansas; his family moved to Oakland during the second Great Migration. He met Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale at the North Oakland Service Center in 1966 and, at sixteen, became the first person to sign on to the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, taking the role of treasurer. On April 6, 1968, two days after the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. in Memphis, an Oakland police shootout near 28th and Union ended with Hutton shot dead after he had emerged with his hands raised; he was seventeen. His funeral at Ephesian Church of God in Christ on April 12, 1968 drew several thousand mourners including Marlon Brando, who delivered a eulogy on the church steps. Independent biographical sourcing for the page is the Bloom and Martin study cited below; this page is added for catalog searchability and does not draw any phrasing from Wikipedia or other tertiary sources.

interview_snippets.speaker: Elmer Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Elmer Dixon, who would help found the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, describes filing past the open casket at Hutton's Oakland funeral, where he learned the casket was open at the request of Hutton's mother:
interview_snippets.quote: And I remember walking by the coffin and I had heard that, you know, in conversation on the way there that Bobby Hutton had been hit by 30 police bullets. And the reason why Dolly Hutton, wanted the coffin to be open, that's what people could see what the police had done to her son.

interview_snippets.speaker: Elmer Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dixon, seventeen at the time, recalls the moment at the funeral he names as his own turning point toward the party:
interview_snippets.quote: And I remember when I was filing Bobby's little Bobby's coffin and seeing him in there, he was 17 years old. And I was 17 years old. And I remember thinking that, you know, that could be me laying there. And I think that was the moment for me where I said, that's it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Aaron Dixon recounts the April 6, 1968 Oakland police shootout, describing the surrender and the killing:
interview_snippets.quote: Little Bobby Hutton throws his rifle out the door, and they come out with their hands up. Little Bobby Hutton is told to run towards a police car. And he does, and they shoot him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the party's internal education program, Dixon uses Hutton as his example of how members learned to read:
interview_snippets.quote: you know, Bobby Hutton didn't know how to read or write before he joined a party. And by the time he's 17, he knows how to read and he knows how to write.

sources.title: Joshua Bloom and Waldo E. Martin Jr., Black against Empire: The History and Politics of the Black Panther Party (the Hutton chapter covers his April 6, 1968 death and the Oakland police-shootout narrative)
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 2013; WorldCat OCLC 811965521
